policy_statement: Implement Salt Lake City FruitShare to divert surplus fruit from local trees to individuals with low access to healthy foods.
mechanism_description: The program diverts surplus fruit to people, avoiding food waste and the emissions associated with producing and disposing of wasted food.
primary_causal_pathway: Surplus edible food diversion → ↓ food waste generation → ↓ landfill/anaerobic decomposition methane + ↓ upstream food production emissions → ↓ net GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Collect and redistribute surplus edible fruit (or other food) to people instead of leaving it unharvested/spoiling or sending it to disposal.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced organic waste decomposition under anaerobic conditions (lower CH₄ generation) and reduced demand for replacement food (lower agricultural production, processing, transport, refrigeration emissions).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower net lifecycle GHG emissions (primarily methane avoidance from food waste and avoided upstream CO₂e from displaced food production).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding net GHG emissions by preventing edible food from entering the waste stream and displacing the need for additional food production.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Surplus food redistribution reduces food waste; this reduces methane from decomposition and reduces upstream emissions by displacing new production → net GHG reduction.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although it improves resource efficiency and social outcomes, the main climate mechanism is direct emissions avoidance (methane + upstream CO₂e), which is Mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If redistribution increases transport/cold storage emissions, the mitigation benefit could be reduced, but the intended and typical dominant effect remains avoided waste and upstream emissions. If food would have been composted/AD anyway, the marginal methane benefit is smaller, but upstream displacement can still dominate.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy with this mechanism (redistributing surplus edible food to prevent waste) is fundamentally aimed at avoiding emissions from wasted food and associated production/disposal; thus Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., landfill methane avoidance vs upstream agricultural emissions avoidance dominance) depend on local baseline disposal pathways and program design and should be assigned at the individual policy evidence level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net emissions benefits depend on baseline counterfactuals (landfill vs compost/AD, existing donation levels), displacement realism (does redistributed food actually reduce other purchases), and added logistics emissions (transport/cold chain). If the program is primarily framed/operated as food security without meaningful waste diversion, climate impact may be secondary, but the canonical mechanism implies waste/emissions avoidance as central.
key_indicators: "FruitShare"; "diverts surplus fruit"; "from local trees"; "to individuals"; "low access to healthy foods"
co_benefits: Improved food security/nutrition; reduced local litter/odor/pest issues from rotting fruit; community engagement/volunteering benefits.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be implemented as a contracted service or logistics/infrastructure support (aggregation/storage), but the text only specifies the diversion program outcome, not specific capital investments or procurement; thus classified as an incentive/programmatic intervention.
